## Definition

**Phospholipid** is introduced in this chapter. It represents a double layer of fat that contains phospholipid molecules. Phospholipids are lipids with a phosphate group attached, and they are one of the main classes of membrane lipids alongside cholesterol (a steroid with a hydroxyl group attached) and glycolipids (lipids with carbohydrate groups attached). In lipid classification, phospholipids fall under *compound lipids* (which also include glycolipids and lipoproteins), as distinguished from *simple lipids* such as fats and waxes.

## Core Principles

Classifier signals: freq_multi_section, bolded_define.

Phospholipids form the basic structural framework of biological membranes — the *lipid bilayer*. The membrane itself consists of lipids and proteins (to which carbohydrates may be attached), with the lipid bilayer providing the foundational architecture into which membrane proteins are embedded. Membrane lipids include phospholipids, cholesterol, and glycolipids, which together compose the bilayer matrix.

Plasma membrane proteins are divided into two categories — *integral proteins* and *peripheral proteins* — both of which interact with the phospholipid bilayer to support membrane function. Most integral proteins are also referred to as *transmembrane proteins*, meaning they span right across the lipid bilayer and are in contact with both the intracellular and extracellular fluid. In contrast, peripheral proteins tend to associate with membrane lipids or integral proteins at either the inner or outer surface of the membrane.

Within the broader lipid classification scheme, phospholipids are categorized as compound lipids (alongside glycolipids and lipoproteins), in contrast to simple lipids such as fats and waxes.

## Sub-concepts

- **Lipid bilayer**: the double-layered phospholipid sheet forming the basic structural framework of the plasma membrane.
- **Other membrane lipids**: cholesterol (steroid with a hydroxyl group) and glycolipids (lipids with carbohydrate groups attached) coexist with phospholipids in the membrane.
- **Membrane proteins** associated with the phospholipid bilayer:
  - *Integral proteins* — most are *transmembrane proteins*, spanning the lipid bilayer and contacting both intracellular and extracellular fluid.
  - *Peripheral proteins* — associate with membrane lipids or integral proteins at either the inner or outer surface of the membrane.
- **Lipid classification context**:
  - *Simple lipids* — fats and waxes.
  - *Compound lipids* — phospholipids, glycolipids, and lipoproteins.

## Practical Applications

Membrane proteins anchored within or onto the phospholipid bilayer carry out a variety of functions essential for maintaining cell viability, illustrating the functional role of the phospholipid bilayer as a platform for protein-mediated cellular processes.
